title: Migrate tests from docker.io
body:
Switch to using images from quay.io/libpod instead, where
we're not (yet) subject to rate limiting.

Disable (skip) one test that I have no idea how to migrate,
because it needs only-one-arch images from docker.io/$arch.

Signed-off-by: Ed Santiago <santiago@redhat.com>

comments.body: @chuanchang @mtrmac could you look at the skipped test and suggest a way to reenable it? I don't understand the point of the test, whether it's to check multiple images, or if it's OK to just check one single image with multiple arches. TIA.

LGTM
I'm fine with getting this in now to get the RHEL gating tests back up and then circling back to fix up the one test.
Oh - I was hoping to get this solved within this PR, before merging. But yes, if this merges with the skip, I will file an issue.

Update, just to show my thinking: `--override-arch` doesn't help. Even with it, skopeo dumps the entire arch list:
```bin
$ bin/skopeo --override-arch=<whatever> inspect --raw docker://quay.io/libpod/busybox:latest |jq .
...long long list
```
where `whatever` can be a real arch (`s390x`) or gibberish (`sdfsdf`).

I suppose we could create a number of `quay.io/libpod/foo_<arch>` images, but it seems wasteful if not needed. I still don't understand the purpose of this test, or, to put it another way, which earlier version of skopeo would have failed this test, and what would that failure look like?
Looks like integration tests need a lot of dockerpocalypse fixes. That's more than I can take on in this PR.
No response. Executive decision: completely rewrite the multiarch manifest test, and make my best effort to describe what it's testing (which I still don't fully understand)

> @chuanchang @mtrmac could you look at the skipped test and suggest a way to reenable it? I don't understand the point of the test, whether it's to check multiple images, or if it's OK to just check one single image with multiple arches. TIA.

I default to thinking that the description is accurate. This came as #746 , probably to test #708 : a failure would be that `skopeo --inspect `**`--raw`** on a manifest list that does not contain the current architecture or OS (most commonly inspecting of a Linux-only image on macOS, but in this case inspecting e.g. an x86+ARM image (no PPC variant) on PPC would fail the same way a non-`--raw` inspect fails, e.g.
```
% bin/skopeo inspect docker://quay.io/libpod/busybox:latest                           
FATA[0002] Error parsing manifest for image: Error choosing image instance: no image found in manifest list for architecture amd64, variant "", OS darwin
```

The current test is, I guess, elegant in that it works without `--override-arch`, but that also makes it complex and with external dependencies. I’d suggest finding a multi-arch image with a limited set of platforms, and testing that `skopeo --override-arch platform_not_on_the_list inspect --raw $image` works. (Actually a literal `platform_not_on_the_list` + any existing multi-arch image would be a valid, although unrealistic, test of the code path.)

> Looks like integration tests need a lot of dockerpocalypse fixes. That's more than I can take on in this PR.

They have been reduced somewhat recently. There is OTOH some definite value in testing that the c/image code works with the Docker Hub; we rely on the integration tests for that. Of course, at some point it just makes sense to say that if they make it too hard to test against, we won’t bother.

> probably to test #708 : a failure would be that skopeo --inspect --raw on a manifest list that does not contain the current architecture or OS

@mtrmac that was exactly what I needed, thank you! Can you see if my new approach looks reasonable?

@edsantiago I concur on the fix the other problem later if it becomes a problem.  More importantly, TYVM for all of your work on the gating test this week.

@jnovy FYI
